title: RELIEF Act
hook: This bill helps non-profit groups offer after-school tutoring and food to K-12 kids.

sections.title: TL;DR

items.label: What
items.text: Creates grants for K-12 after-school tutoring and food programs.

items.label: Who
items.text: Sponsor information not available in provided text.

items.label: Status
items.text: Status unknown based on provided text.

sections.expanded_detail: This bill aims to create a new grant program within the Department of Education. It would provide money to non-profit organizations that offer after-school tutoring and nutritional support to K-12 students, especially in low-income communities. The bill text does not include details on its sponsor or current legislative status.

sections.title: Where Is This Bill?
sections.expanded_detail: This bill has been introduced in Congress. It now needs to be assigned to a committee for review and potential revisions. If it passes committee, it would then be eligible for a vote in the House of Representatives.

sections.title: If This Passes, You Might Notice

bullets: More K-12 students could get after-school tutoring.
More K-12 students could receive nutritional support after school.
Non-profit groups could apply for federal money for these programs.

sections.expanded_detail: If passed, K-12 students, especially those in low-income areas, could gain access to free after-school tutoring to help with their studies. They might also receive nutritious food, addressing potential hunger after school hours. Non-profit organizations meeting specific criteria would be able to apply for federal grants to fund and expand these vital services, starting in fiscal year 2026.

sections.title: The Debate

collapsed_summary.supporters: This bill would help families and children in need by funding critical after-school support programs.
collapsed_summary.critics: No specific arguments against this bill are available in the provided text.

sections.expanded_detail: The bill's findings state that many families struggle with the rising costs of childcare and primary education, especially in low-income urban areas. The grant program is designed to ease these burdens by supporting K-12 students through academic help and food. No specific criticisms or additional viewpoints are detailed within the bill text itself.

sections.title: Bottom Line
sections.collapsed_summary: This bill creates a grant program to help non-profits provide after-school tutoring and food to K-12 kids.
sections.expanded_detail: The bill authorizes $50 million annually for five years, from 2026 to 2030, to fund this program. Its success would depend on non-profit participation and effective implementation by the Department of Education, targeting organizations with at least three years of operation in areas where 40% of residents live below the poverty line.
